evals.prompt:
Apply the ms style guide pass to this blog excerpt:

---
I've been thinking about how we set up our CI pipeline, and the first sanity check I run every time is whether the master branch is actually clean. Not "pretty clean" — actually clean. No uncommitted changes, no unstaged files, nothing. The guys on my team learned this the hard way when a blacklist of bad commit patterns slipped through and the build blew up in production.

The fix was pretty straightforward: 3 steps, and you're done. First, you hit the pipeline config. Then you set up a dummy webhook endpoint to receive events. Third, you log in to the dashboard and validate the allowlist is populated.

It's insane how often teams skip this sanity check. We've been burned by it enough times that it's now just part of how we work. If you're not doing this, you're basically flying blind.
---
evals.expected_output: Draft with: blacklist→blocklist (if present), sanity check→soundness check or similar, master branch flagged or noted, guys→people/team, dummy→sample/placeholder, hit→select/press, 3→three, straightforward flagged, flying blind flagged. Voice preserved.

assertions.name: blacklist-replaced
assertions.check: The word 'blacklist' does not appear in the edited draft (unless in a changelog note about the original)

assertions.name: sanity-check-replaced
assertions.check: 'sanity check' is replaced with a bias-free alternative

assertions.name: guys-replaced
assertions.check: 'The guys' or 'guys on my team' is replaced with a neutral alternative like 'the team' or 'people'

assertions.name: number-three-spelled-out
assertions.check: The numeral '3' for count of steps is spelled out as 'three' in the body text

assertions.name: changelog-present
assertions.check: A 'Style pass changes' section appears after the edited draft listing what was changed

assertions.name: voice-preserved
assertions.check: First-person voice ('I've been', 'we learned') is preserved unchanged

evals.prompt:
Run the ms style pass on this AI blog section:

---
When you're building an agent that needs to hit an external API, the first thing you need is a solid allowlist of domains your agent is allowed to call. Without that, you're essentially giving the model free reign to call anything on the Internet — which is insane from a security standpoint.

I built a dummy proxy layer to sit between my agent and the real endpoints. It logs every call, validates the response format, and kills the request if anything looks off. The proxy runs as a master process with 4 worker threads under it. If a worker dies, the master restarts it automatically.

Here's the thing about building these systems: it's not easy. Most guys who try to bolt on security after the fact end up with a crippled architecture that's harder to audit than if they'd started from scratch. The sanity check I use before any deployment is running 10 test scenarios against a dummy environment. If all 10 pass, I'm confident enough to ship.
---
evals.expected_output: sanity check→soundness/validation, dummy→sample/placeholder, guys→people/developers, crippled→broken/limited, master process noted (may keep as technical term with note), kills→stops/cancels, Internet→internet, insane flagged. Voice preserved.

assertions.name: sanity-check-replaced
assertions.check: 'sanity check' is replaced or flagged

assertions.name: crippled-replaced
assertions.check: 'crippled architecture' is replaced with a bias-free alternative

assertions.name: internet-lowercase
assertions.check: 'Internet' is changed to 'internet' (mid-sentence)

assertions.name: dummy-replaced
assertions.check: 'dummy environment' and/or 'dummy proxy' are replaced with 'sample' or 'placeholder'

assertions.name: guys-replaced
assertions.check: 'Most guys who' is replaced with a neutral alternative

assertions.name: changelog-present
assertions.check: A changelog section appears after the draft

assertions.name: master-process-handled
assertions.check: The 'master process' term is either changed with a note or explicitly flagged in the changelog as a judgment call (technical term vs. deprecated usage)

evals.prompt:
Ms style pass on this:

---
Setting up a login flow for the first time always feels like a right of passage. You start with the login page, the user logs-in, you validate the token, and then — if everything checks out — you redirect them. Simple.

Except it's never simple. The first sanity check is whether your blacklist of revoked tokens is actually being checked before the redirect, not after. I've seen 5 teams make this mistake. In every case, the guy who built the original login didn't think about token revocation until someone raised it during a security review. By then, the set-up was already baked into 3 different services and untangling it was a nightmare.

The fix I recommend: whitelist your trusted redirect domains explicitly. Don't rely on a blacklist of bad patterns — those are brittle and you'll miss edge cases. Maintain an allowlist. Check it on every request. Log every login attempt to your e-mail or your logging setup. It's 9 lines of code.
---
evals.expected_output: blacklist→blocklist (x2), whitelist→allowlist, sanity check→soundness check, login/log in verb/noun distinction applied, set-up→setup (noun), e-mail→email, guy→person/developer, 5→five, 3→three, 9→nine. Voice preserved.

assertions.name: blacklist-replaced
assertions.check: Both instances of 'blacklist' are replaced

assertions.name: whitelist-replaced
assertions.check: 'whitelist' is replaced with 'allowlist'

assertions.name: email-hyphen-removed
assertions.check: 'e-mail' is changed to 'email'

assertions.name: login-verb-noun
assertions.check: 'logs-in' is corrected to 'logs in' (verb) and 'login page' stays as 'login' (noun/adjective)

assertions.name: numbers-spelled-out
assertions.check: Small numbers like 5, 3, 9 are spelled out where they refer to counts (five teams, three services, nine lines)

assertions.name: changelog-present
assertions.check: A changelog section appears after the draft

assertions.name: voice-preserved
assertions.check: First-person phrases like 'I've seen', 'The fix I recommend' are preserved
